title: Intelligent Systems for Screen Archives
thumbnail: ../img/project/preview/issa-thumb.jpg || Interface of a search tool on a laptop screen surrounded by post-it notes
hero: ../img/project/issa/insitu.jpg || Interface of a search tool on a laptop screen surrounded by post-it notes
rationale: Interaction system for semantic discovery
oneliner: Intent-driven AI search system for exploring film archives beyond keyword logic
lead: Led design of semantic interaction model enabling intent-based exploration of archival data
context: Film archives hold enormous creative potential, but existing tools require users to define what they are looking for upfront. This creates friction for filmmakers and researchers who often begin with a vague idea, mood, or reference. ISSA explored how archive systems could move beyond query-based search toward intent-guided discovery, where the system adapts to the user’s goal rather than the other way around.
approach: I led the design of a conceptual framework and interface for an intent-guided archive system, working closely with the Principal Investigator. The core contribution was a 9-step workflow that moves from understanding the archive to expressing intent, interpreting system output, steering results, reconstructing context, and producing usable material. The system was designed to make AI interpretation visible and adjustable at every stage, shifting the user role from searcher to collaborator.
impact: The project introduced a new interaction model for archive systems, shifting from retrieval to interpretation. It established a shared design direction for ongoing research and demonstrated how machine learning could support creative workflows rather than constrain them.
detail_1: Starting from the limitation of search || Faceted search works well when you know your parameters. But creative users often begin with a feeling, a reference, or a project type. Existing archive tools offer no entry point for this kind of vague intent. ISSA proposed an alternative: start from the objective, and let the system configure itself around it. My role was to make that proposition legible and usable. || || ||
detail_2: Understanding existing archive tools and interfaces || I reviewed a range of film archive tools to understand how collections are currently explored. Most rely on structured search, metadata filters, and catalogue-style navigation, supporting known-item retrieval but limiting exploratory use. This revealed a consistent gap: systems are optimised for precision, not interpretation. This reinforced the need for an interface that supports ambiguity, iteration, and creative intent. || ../img/project/issa/archive-tools.jpg || Screenshots of various film archive search interfaces || Existing archive interfaces prioritise structured retrieval over exploration
detail_3: Understanding the system || Working with engineers, I explored how video was being processed and segmented through machine learning pipelines. Three approaches were being developed, each producing a different interpretation of the same material: Semantic segmentation (MVP1), Visual scene detection (MVP2), and Audio-visual interpretation (MVP3). These approaches showed that segmentation is not fixed but interpretative. This shaped the interface: the system needed to expose its reasoning, support comparison, and allow users to challenge outputs. || ../img/project/issa/backend-workflow.jpg || Diagram showing video segmentation pipelines and workflows || Flowchart of the backend approaches
detail_4: Early interface explorations || Early drafts explored how these different system interpretations could be translated into a usable experience. The focus was on structuring complexity, exposing system logic, and introducing points where users could guide or challenge the output. || ../img/project/issa/interface-drafts.jpg || Early interface layout explorations for archive system || Initial interface explorations testing structure and interaction
detail_5: Transparency as a design principle  || The system is structured as a 9 step guided workflow, moving from orientation to creation. Each step balances system intelligence with user control, allowing interpretation to be surfaced, challenged, and shaped. It treats AI as visible and negotiable. At every stage, users can see what the system detected, how confident it is, and what alternatives exist. Interaction is continuous rather than reset-based, turning disagreement into a productive part of the process.|| ../img/project/issa/9stepworkflow.jpg || flowchart of 9 steps || 9 step workflow moving from orientation to creation
detail_6: Using AI tools to design an AI system || Mockups were produced using Claude and Figma Make, enabling rapid iteration at concept stage. This approach reflected the system itself: AI accelerates exploration, but the human remains in control of direction and intent. || || ||
detail_7: What exists and what comes next || This demonstrator establishes the interaction model for an intent-guided archive system currently in development. Presented at the ISSA event, it introduced the framework to researchers and archive professionals who will shape its future direction. || || ||

title: Ageing and Gender in European Cinema
thumbnail: ../img/project/preview/agec-thumb.jpg || Person holding a tablet showing a data exploration search interface
hero: ../img/project/agec/insitu.jpg  || Person holding a tablet showing a data exploration search interface
rationale: Interactive system enabling structured exploration of archival cinema data
oneliner: Research tool for exploring 50 years of European cinema data through an interactive interface
partners: Goethe-Universität::https://www.goethe-university-frankfurt.de || Universitatea Babeș-Bolyai::https://www.ubbcluj.ro/en || Università degli Studi di Udine::https://www.uniud.it/en || King's College London::https://www.kcl.ac.uk || Université Sorbonne Nouvelle::https://www.sorbonne-nouvelle.fr/en/
lead: Worked across four European institutions on a constrained part-time schedule shaping interaction structure from early ambiguity to implementation
context: An international research project studying age and gender representation across European cinema. The dataset (films, biographies, roles spanning over 50 years) needed to be transformed from researcher spreadsheets into an interactive exploration tool usable by academics, film programmers, and policy makers alike.
approach: I led design from early discovery through to completion, structuring the work around progressive clarification of an initially undefined dataset. A structured kickoff questionnaire aligned a distributed academic team upfront, followed by visual ideation, user flows, and iterative wireframing to establish interaction patterns before data was fully available. Key decisions included separating exploration from refinement through a persistent filter drawer, unifying films and biographies into a single result model, and developing a flexible chart system that supports multiple visualisations alongside accessible alternatives such as tabular data. Throughout, design decisions were grounded in both evolving data realities and technical constraints.
impact: Established a flexible exploration tool that translates a complex, multi-decade dataset into a system that supports both structured analysis and open-ended discovery. The work creates a foundation for researchers to generate insights, visual outputs, and exportable data that feed directly into publications, while remaining accessible to broader audiences such as programmers and policy makers. It also sets a scalable model for extending the platform as the dataset and research evolve.
detail_1: Starting with a better brief || The usual project kickoff is a meeting with notes that don't capture enough. For AGE-C, I introduced a structured questionnaire sent to the academic team before any design work began, covering objectives, audience, device and browser requirements, accessibility considerations, and crucially, questions that pushed clients to distinguish wants from needs. With 12 external stakeholders across four European institutions, getting alignment in writing before the conversation was essential. It proved more useful than any meeting would have been. || || ||
detail_2: Designing for three different audiences at once || The tool needed to serve primary users (film scholars and gerontology researchers using it to generate data for articles), secondary users (film and festival programmers browsing for curation), and tertiary users (including policy makers and general public). Those audiences have very different mental models of what a film database looks like. Benchmarking against IMDB and Rotten Tomatoes informed how film and biography data could be displayed together rather than separately, which simplified the information architecture significantly. || || ||
detail_3: Ideating without data || The academic team was still collecting data when design work began. I gathered visual references of interactive dashboards, age and gender charts, and data-above-results interfaces to understand what patterns might translate well. These were shared with the team to prompt discussion and expand the range of ideas, helping us explore possible directions before committing to a single approach. || ../img/project/agec/ideation-references.jpg || Collection of screenshots showing charts, dashboards and search interfaces used as visual research references || Visual research references used to explore chart types, dashboards, and search patterns
detail_4: Aligning structure through user flows and architecture || I mapped out user flows and information architecture early to align both the internal and external teams. While not visually expressive, this step was critical in clarifying user touchpoints, system structure, and technical constraints before moving into interface design. Sharing these diagrams created a common reference point for discussion, ensuring that design decisions remained grounded in both user needs and system feasibility. || ../img/project/agec/userflow-ia.jpg || Diagram showing user flow and information architecture for a data exploration tool || User flow and information architecture used to align design and technical structure
detail_5: Understanding the data through chart experimentation || Before finalising the interface, I explored the dataset through chart experimentation. Using tools such as Flourish (beta), and occasionally RAWGraphs or Excel, I tested different visualisations to understand how the data behaved and what forms communicated it most clearly. This stage helped shape decisions around chart types, hierarchy, and interaction, grounding visual design choices in the actual structure and limitations of the data. || ../img/project/agec/chart-experiments.jpg || Examples of data visualisation experiments created using charting tools such as Flourish || Early chart experiments used to understand data shape and clarity
detail_6: Structuring the interface through wireframes || Wireframes were used extensively to test layout and interaction without visual distraction. Early iterations explored a single-page interface, but this quickly became overloaded as filters increased. Moving filters into a slide-out drawer created a clearer separation between exploration and refinement. These low-fidelity iterations made it easier to communicate changes with partners and evaluate trade-offs before committing to development. || ../img/project/agec/wireframe-landing.jpg || Wireframe showing landing page layout and early iteration of filter interface || Landing page wireframe showing early layout and filter exploration
detail_7: Evolving the chart system under constraints || Due to time constraints, we implemented charts using a JavaScript library, selecting Unovis for its flexibility and range of chart types. Accessibility informed several decisions: adding descriptive titles and annotations, providing a tabular data alternative, and switching from vertical to horizontal bar charts for better responsiveness on smaller screens. || ../img/project/agec/chart-wireframes.jpg || Wireframes showing chart layouts and iterations including horizontal bar charts and accessibility considerations || Chart wireframes exploring layout, accessibility, and responsiveness
detail_8: Expanding functionality through iteration || Later iterations expanded both the range of data variables and the functionality available to users. Additional chart types were introduced alongside options to download visualisations (SVG), access underlying data tables, and export datasets (CSV). These additions supported different user needs, from quick exploration to deeper analysis and reuse of the data beyond the platform. || ../img/project/agec/chart-iterations.jpg || Interface showing multiple chart types and options to download visualisations and data || Iterations expanding chart types, data variables, and export functionality
detail_9: What the project is teaching || Working at under 1.5 days a month across a six-month timeline with a large international team is a specific kind of design challenge, high-stakes decisions made infrequently, with long gaps between touchpoints. It's sharpened how I structure design decisions to be legible to a team that isn't living with them day to day. || || ||

title: Little Pictures
thumbnail: ../img/project/preview/littlepictures-thumb.jpg || Minimal graphical climate data visualisations on a tablet
hero: ../img/project/littlepictures/insitu.jpg || Minimal graphical climate data visualisations on a tablet
rationale: Data translated into accessible visual narratives
oneliner: Accessible climate data visualisations designed to communicate complex datasets
lead: Designed data narratives to make complex climate datasets legible and accessible across audiences
context: This self-initiated project explored how climate data visualisations could be made more accessible and sustainable, particularly for screen reader users. It emerged from a desire to move beyond conventional charting approaches and rethink how data could be structured and presented with minimal dependencies.
approach: I reframed the visualisation problem by treating the HTML table as the core data structure, layering multiple visual outputs through CSS and lightweight JavaScript. Through iterative prototyping, I explored different representations of the same dataset, balancing clarity, accessibility, and performance while working within intentionally constrained design parameters.
impact: This project demonstrates an alternative approach to data visualisation that prioritises accessibility and sustainability from the outset. It informed my broader approach to building lightweight, inclusive systems, and highlights how constraint-led design can produce flexible and scalable solutions.
detail_1: Inspiration from the Little Pictures Project || I have been wanting to work with climate data for a while but never felt confident, until now. After a busy period, I began exploring datasets and discovered the Little Pictures project from the ESA Climate Office. The simplicity of the visuals, open-source nature, and accessible data made it an ideal starting point. || ../img/project/littlepictures/gallery.jpg || Gallery of colourful climate visualisations || Screenshot of images from the ESA Climate Office Little Pictures Gallery
detail_2: Shifting focus to accessibility and sustainability || I knew from reading articles how difficult it is to make data visualisation accessible for screen reader users. The concept evolved into using a single HTML table per dataset, styled in multiple ways, allowing both accessibility and visual exploration without duplication. || || ||
detail_3: Designing with intentional constraints || To maintain focus on the project’s core concepts, I deliberately limited the design. I avoided gradients, used a black-and-white palette, and incorporated only simple elements. || ../img/project/littlepictures/illustrator.jpg || Illustrator file showing layout explorations || Layout ideas, inspiration and mockups
detail_4: Prototyping visual formats || I explored multiple visual representations including bar charts, line charts, and layouts, all generated from the same dataset. || ../img/project/littlepictures/graph-prototypes.jpg || Different graph prototypes including bar and line charts || Graph prototypes
detail_5: Development challenges and iteration || Styling the bar chart was straightforward, but the line chart presented challenges, particularly with responsiveness and alignment. Integrating datasets required simplifying data and adapting JavaScript for flexibility, involving repeated iteration and testing. || || ||

title: Family Circle
thumbnail: ../img/project/preview/familycircle-thumb.jpg || A colourful circle made of yarn
hero: ../img/project/familycircle/annotated.jpg  || A colourful circle made of yarn
rationale: Relational dataset expressed through visual form
oneliner: Generational data visualisation exploring family relationships and storytelling
lead: Developed visual systems to explore relational family data structures and narrative patterns
context: Using leftover materials, this project became an opportunity to revisit data visualisation in a more tactile, personal form, building on a previous project with my children. The focus shifted from digital representation to something physical, relational, and grounded in making.
approach: Explored how generational data could be translated into a physical system using colour, sequence, and repetition. Rather than defining everything upfront, the structure evolved through sketching, testing, and making, with decisions shaped by material behaviour and simplicity. The process balanced intention with openness, allowing the form to emerge through iteration.
impact: Resulted in a physical representation of family data that is both visual and experiential. It created a shared activity that combined making, reflection, and storytelling, strengthening connections through the process itself. More broadly, it demonstrates how data visualisation can extend beyond screens into material and relational forms.
detail_1: Crafting a Family Bond || This was spontaneous weekend project which came about when my colleague offered some cotton string cutoffs from hand weaving a kākahu, a traditional Māori cloak she was lovingly weaving for her sister with beautiful patterns, colours and finished with feathers. Inspired by the cultural significance of kākahu as family heirlooms, I thought it would be a nice opportunity to craft something with my kids, connecting it to our family. It had been four years since we last made a data visualisation together, and I was ready to try another one. The activity needed to be engaging, relatable, and simple enough for my children, while remaining manageable for me.|| ../img/project/familycircle/kakahu.jpg || White blue and yellow pattern weaving with different stitches and white feathers at the bottom || Close up of kākahu, photo courtesy of Samantha Callaghan
detail_2: Translating Family Structure into Visual Form || After brainstorming and searching for ideas with my children, we settled on creating round macrame-inspired pieces that could double as coasters. Each person would knot their age number in their favourite colour, starting with the children, followed by the parents. Initial sketches engaged the kids, leading to discussions about colours and counting age markers for family members. Seeing their enthusiasm, I decided to include grandparents to expand the project. Although we initially considered showing two family sides merging, it was too complex, so we opted for a simpler sequence from youngest to oldest. || ../img/project/familycircle/sketches.jpg || Four colorful circles, each with a different design, representing a vibrant array of patterns and colors. || Sketches on paper and on sketching app
detail_3: Refining Design Through Constraints and Collaborations ||   Macrame wasn’t suitable for the spiral pattern I envisioned due to its complexity and need for longer strings. Switching to a wrapping method offered simplicity and allowed us to add frayed sections, a detail the kids loved, to indicate male or female. After testing, we decided on frayed edges for females and knots for males. Their father suggested placing frayed edges at the start or end of sections to balance the design.
detail_4: Structuring the Making Process || To manage the process, the work was broken down into clear steps including labelling colours, preparing string quantities, dyeing, drying, wrapping based on age, and assembling the final form. This created a simple system that balanced planning with flexibility, allowing the making process to unfold in stages. || ../img/project/familycircle/calculation.jpg || A child focused on writing on paper with a pencil, showcasing concentration in calculating mathematic formulas. || Calculating the amount of strings to dye and number of wraps
detail_5: Working with Colour and Variation || Dyeing introduced an element of unpredictability, with colours shifting in intensity once dried. Adjustments and re-dyeing became part of the process, highlighting the gap between intention and outcome when working with physical materials. || ../img/project/familycircle/dyeing.jpg || Three children putting string into colored liquid into jars with spoons, illustrating a fun moment of exploration. || Getting the colours needed by dyeing string
detail_6: Observing Material Behaviour Over Time || The transformation from wet to dry revealed changes in colour vibrancy that required reconsideration of earlier decisions, reinforcing the importance of patience and timing when working with dyed materials. || ../img/project/familycircle/dried-string.jpg || A collection of colorful yarns displayed in jars on a white table, showcasing various textures and hues. || Dried string in the various colours and amounts
detail_7: Assembling and Reflection || The repetitive nature of the task of counting each wrap created moments of reflection, particularly when working through older generations, connecting the act of making with the passage of time. || ../img/project/familycircle/wrapping.jpg || A child perched on a chair used as a frame for wrapping string, enveloped a blanket, unknotting string || Wrapping on a makeshift frame with someone helping me unknot
detail_8: Resolving Structure Through Improvisation || Final assembly proved more complex than expected, with the structure requiring additional support. While sewing was initially attempted, the use of glue became necessary to stabilise the piece, reflecting a pragmatic balance between ideal process and functional outcome. || ../img/project/familycircle/wrapped-string.jpg || A long bright string of wrapped yarn in various colors rests on a wooden floor || Wrapped string
detail_9: From Data Representation to Shared Artefact || The completed piece brought together data, material, and shared effort into a single object, representing both the structure of the family and the experience of making it. It functions not only as a visualisation but also as a record of time spent together, with potential to exist as a small heirloom. || ../img/project/familycircle/annotated.jpg || A colorful circle made of yarn symbolizing continuity and connection ||

title: Red Riding Hood 2
thumbnail: ../img/project/preview/rrh2-thumb.jpg || Desktop monitor and tablet device showing a data visualisation and pages of a book
hero: ../img/project/rrh2/insitu.jpg || Desktop monitor and tablet device showing a data visualisation and pages of a book
rationale: Analytical interpretation of narrative structure
oneliner: Emotional and sentiment analysis integrated into narrative data visualisation
lead: Worked with layered narrative datasets combining sentiment analysis with visual storytelling systems
context: This project sits between personal development and applied experimentation and builds on a previous data visualisation exploration, using it as a foundation to deepen both technical and conceptual approaches. It focuses on reworking an existing dataset to explore alternative representations, particularly around how emotional and linguistic data could be represented more meaningfully. It became an opportunity to deepen technical and visual skills while experimenting with more complex narrative structures and analytical layers.
approach: I combined manual data processes, sentiment and emotion analysis, and iterative prototyping to evolve the visual system. Moving from static design to coded implementation, I explored how CSS-led approaches could support responsive, interactive storytelling while balancing clarity with density of information. Iteration, reflection, and external feedback played a key role in shaping both the visual system and interaction model.
impact: The project significantly advanced my approach to data visualisation, particularly in integrating multiple analytical layers into a cohesive system. It demonstrated how narrative clarity can be strengthened through interaction design, extending design thinking, enabling more flexible, responsive, and exploratory ways of engaging with complex datasets. It also established a more sustainable and transferable workflow for future projects.
detail_1: Elevating Data Visualisation Skills and Expanding Prior Work || Since I learnt a lot from my last project, I started a new data visualisation course with Frederica Fragapane to further develop my skills and explore new approaches. Rather than starting from scratch, I revisited the same dataset to push it further and explore ideas I hadn’t been able to implement previously. || ../img/project/rrh2viz/page1-book-viz.jpg || Comparison of original book page and visualisation || Scan of original page and corresponding visualisation
detail_2: Incorporating Sentiment Analysis and Exploring Techniques || Building on earlier feedback, I explored ways to integrate sentiment analysis more meaningfully into the visual system. I experimented with representing all words in circular layouts to retain positional meaning while maintaining a minimal visual language. || ../img/project/rrh2/word-circles.jpg || Experiments with circular layouts and sentiment visualisation || Exploring alternative visual representations
detail_3: Reevaluating Sentiment Analysis Tools || I revisited the sentiment analysis process and identified inconsistencies in previous tools, leading me to switch to Twinword for more accurate and efficient results. || ../img/project/rrh2/sentiment-comparison.jpg || Comparison of sentiment analysis outputs || Comparing outputs from different sentiment tools
detail_4: Sketching Ideas and Data Wrangling || I explored multiple layouts and data transformations, including adapting RAWgraphs outputs to create circular word structures. || ../img/project/rrh2/sketchbook.jpg || Sketchbook drawings and ideas || Concept sketches and layout exploration
detail_5: Prototyping with CSS and CodePen || I transitioned from Illustrator into code, using CSS and CodePen to create responsive and interactive prototypes. Iterations focused on simplifying interactions while maintaining the integrity of the visual system. || ../img/project/rrh2/illustrator-codepen.jpg || Design and prototype comparisons || From static mockups to coded prototypes
detail_6: Iteration, Feedback and Refinement || Through peer feedback and participation in a Data Visualisation Society session, I refined navigation, clarity, and storytelling elements. || ../img/project/rrh2/final-screenshot.jpg || Prototype overview || Prototype shared during feedback session

title: Digital Accessibility Reimagined
thumbnail: ../img/project/preview/digitalaccessibility-thumb.jpg || Person in front of sunlit bay windows holding a poster
hero: ../img/project/digitalaccessibility/insitu.jpg  || Person in front of sunlit bay windows holding a poster
rationale: Governance and structural behavioural change
oneliner: Shifted accessibility from compliance task to distributed organisational responsibility
lead: Initiated organisational change embedding accessibility into shared delivery workflows across disciplines
context: Digital accessibility at King's Digital Lab began as a compliance-driven effort across a large portfolio of projects, but lacked a clear structure for how the work should be carried out across roles. The result was inconsistent delivery and low adoption. This project focused on redesigning the process itself to make accessibility workable and sustainable across the team.
approach: I led the redesign of the digital accessibility process by aligning it with the lab's Software Development Life Cycle, defining role-based responsibilities, and testing the approach across live projects. The goal was to shift from fragmented, role-heavy effort to a shared system the team could realistically adopt and sustain.
impact: The work was accepted at conferences (RSECon2025 and DH2026), and shared not just as a report of what changed, but as an invitation for others to build on, adapt, or challenge the approach. It contributed to wider sector discussions on collective responsibility for digital accessibility in research software teams.
detail_1: Establishing a shared understanding and direction || In 2023, I initiated and led the formation of a small dedicated digital accessibility team (designer, engineer, project manager) to address these structural issues. Rather than introducing a prescriptive framework, the work began by making the problem visible and shared across the team. Workshops, discussions, and iterative documentation helped surface where the existing approach was breaking down, while aligning different perspectives. This created a foundation for change based on evidence rather than assumption. || ../img/project/digitalaccessibility/miro-iterations.jpg || Miro board showing multiple iterations of digital accessibility process diagrams with comments and feedback from team members over time || Iterative process development through shared workshops and feedback
detail_2: Reframing digital accessibility as a shared responsibility || A key shift was moving digital accessibility away from being treated as a specialist task toward a shared responsibility across roles. Previous approaches had concentrated the majority of the work within design and development, which was neither sustainable nor aligned with how projects functioned in practice. Inclusive design frameworks helped make digital accessibility more tangible and relatable, supporting clearer conversations about how each role contributes without overloading any single discipline. || ../img/project/digitalaccessibility/responsibility-roles.jpg || Illustrated avatars showing different roles responsible for accessibility tasks across a project lifecycle from https://www.ontario.ca/page/inclusive-design-toolkit || Defining accessibility as a distributed responsibility across roles
detail_3: Embedding digital accessibility into the development lifecycle || With a shared understanding in place, the focus shifted to structure. Digital accessibility was aligned with the Software Development Life Cycle, mapping responsibilities to specific phases so they became visible, actionable, and easier to adopt within existing workflows. This ensured each role understood what they were responsible for, when it should happen, and how it connected to the wider delivery process. || ../img/project/digitalaccessibility/sdlc-integration.jpg || Diagram showing digital accessibility steps integrated into the software development lifecycle stages || Digital accessibility integrated into the lab’s existing development lifecycle
detail_4: Making requirements usable in practice || Early attempts to define digital accessibility requirements followed existing prioritisation models, but these proved difficult to apply in practice. The distinction between levels of work was unclear and did not support day-to-day decision making. This led to the development of a more practical model based on roles, responsibilities, and time expectations, making requirements clearer and more usable within real project constraints. || ../img/project/digitalaccessibility/requirements-flow.jpg || Flowchart showing accessibility requirement levels and decision pathways based on project needs || One of the early requirements flowchart models that practical decision tools
detail_5: Testing and refining through real projects || The process was tested across multiple projects of varying scale and complexity. Rather than validating in theory, it was shaped through real constraints, timelines, and team dynamics. These trials revealed gaps in clarity, prioritisation, and communication, leading to further refinement. Iteration ensured the process was not only defined, but workable in practice. || || ||
detail_6: Extending the approach to long-term maintenance || Integrating digital accessibility into ongoing maintenance introduced new challenges. Projects varied in age, structure, and documentation, making consistency difficult. The revised approach began to define how digital accessibility could be incorporated into service-level agreements, shifting from one-off compliance efforts toward a more sustainable, long-term model. || ../img/project/digitalaccessibility/maintain-sla-flow.jpg || Flowchart showing digital accessibility considerations during maintenance stage under service level agreements || Extending digital accessibility into long-term maintenance workflows
detail_7: From internal process to shared contribution || The work culminated in a documented framework that could be shared beyond the lab. Presented at RSEConf 2025 and prepared for DH2026, it contributed to broader discussions on embedding digital accessibility within research software teams. || ../img/project/digitalaccessibility/rse-poster.jpg || Conference poster presenting the accessibility process and framework developed at King's Digital Lab || Poster presented and won an award at RSEConf 2025, with ongoing iteration

title: Redesigning Organisational Systems
thumbnail: ../img/project/preview/redesigningorg-thumb.jpg || Tablet device on a table showing an organisation website project page
hero: ../img/project/redesigningorg/insitu.jpg  || Tablet device on a table showing an organisation website project page
rationale: Organisational workflow and content architecture transformation
oneliner: Scaled a content system from 18 to 80 published projects through structural redesign
lead: Led structural redesign of publishing workflows across a growing organisational content system
context: King's Digital Lab's website had only 18 project entries despite a much larger portfolio. The content management system was underused because it was time-consuming, offered too many choices without guidance, and produced inconsistent results. The system was working against the people who needed to use it. While the redesign spanned multiple parts of the site, this case study focuses specifically on project pages as a representative slice of the wider system.
approach: I led the redesign end to end, as an internal product transformation, starting with a structured discovery phase. Working with a core team, I introduced an evidence-led process to align polarised opinions. The redesign automated fields where data already existed elsewhere, tightened the content model to reduce choice overload and manual effort. Process guidelines were introduced to clarify contribution expectations, and enabled project pages to function as a reliable, scalable system.
impact: Project pages proved an example for broader system redesign. They repositioned the website as an internal content platform rather than a static showcase. The redesign transformed an underused content system into a reliable consistent organisational platform. Published projects grew from 18 to 80, a 344% increase, with a 75% reduction in manual input. More importantly, established sustainable contribution habits across the team, creating a scalable foundation for ongoing growth and visibility of the lab’s work.
detail_1: Recognising a systems problem, not a content problem || The low entry count wasn't because people didn't have projects to add, it was because the system made it too hard to do it. Too many fields, too little guidance, and no clear distinction between what was required and what was optional. The result was that people either didn't contribute at all, or contributed inconsistently. Before designing anything, I needed to understand exactly where the friction was.
detail_2: Replacing opinion with evidence || Internal discussions were highly polarised between team members. There were strong views about what the site should be and do. To move beyond opinion-led decisions and mediate preferences, I established a shared evidence base through heuristic evaluation, analytics review, competitor benchmarking, and a full content audit. Workshops, documented discussions, and regular updates ensured transparency and maintained team cohesion, shifting the conversation from subjective opinions to observable patterns and constraints. || ../img/project/redesigningorg/research-analysis.jpg || Screens showing heuristic evaluation, analytics insights, benchmarking and content audit materials || Research and analysis used to align decisions across a multi-stakeholder team
detail_3: Designing the system, not just the interface || The core design decision wasn't visual, it was structural. The redesign required coordination across design, engineering, and lab operations. While engineers developed data pipelines and models, I translated system capabilities into interface and interaction patterns through wireframes and strategic planning. This ensured that the interface reflected how the system actually worked: what could be automated, what required input, and how contributors would realistically engage with it over time. || ../img/project/redesigningorg/strategy-wireframes.jpg || Screens showing strategic planning, data models and wireframe layouts || Connecting data models, system logic, and interface design
detail_4: Reducing friction at the point of entry || The original project page required extensive manual input, with many mandatory fields and little guidance, creating friction, inconsistency and discouraging contribution. I worked through the information architecture to separate what could be automated (project title, status, dates, funders, team members pulled from existing data) from what required manual input (one image with alt text, a project description). || ../img/project/redesigningorg/old-project-page.jpg || screenshot of webpage to the left and long list of requirements to the right || New project page showing simplified structure with automated fields and reduced manual input
detail_5: Reduction to removing friction || I redesigned the content model to automate known data and reduce mandatory inputs to a minimal set. Contributors no longer faced a system with around 8 options and an equally long list of optionals; they faced a clear, much shorter set of 2 mandatory inputs and a small number of optional enhancements. Contributors now engaged with a lightweight, guided structure where effort is focused only where it adds value.  || ../img/project/redesigningorg/new-project-page.jpg || screenshot of webpage to the left and long list of requirements to the right || After: Redesigned project page with automated and reduced input fields. Simplified, partially automated, and easier to maintain
detail_6: Designing for adoption and long-term use || The success of the system depended on adoption, not just usability. Contribution guidelines were co-developed with the team to reflect real workflows, supported by regular updates and open feedback loops. This ensured the system was understood, trusted, and integrated into everyday practice rather than treated as an additional task. || || ||
detail_7: Behaviour change as the real outcome || The most significant shift was behavioural. The platform moved from being avoided to being actively used, becoming a central and reliable source of organisational knowledge rather than an afterthought. || || ||

title: Living with Machines
thumbnail: ../img/project/preview/livingwithmachines-thumb.jpg || Two computer screens showing line graphs and scatter plots
hero: ../img/project/livingwithmachines/insitu.jpg  || Two computer screens showing line graphs and scatter plots
rationale: Large-scale dataset interpretation through visual form
oneliner: Public data exploration of 19th-century historical narratives through visualisation
output: Language of Mechanisation notebook::https://observablehq.com/@jmiguelv/language-of-mechanisation || Language of Accidents notebook::https://observablehq.com/@jmiguelv/lwm-language-of-accidents
partners: The Alan Turing Institute::https://www.turing.ac.uk || British Library::https://www.bl.uk || Queen Mary University of London::https://www.qmul.ac.uk || King's College London::https://www.kcl.ac.uk
lead: Designed narrative visualisation systems for large-scale historical datasets across academic research
context: A large-scale interdisciplinary research programme exploring 19th-century industrialisation through newspaper data. The challenge was translating complex datasets and research questions into tools that could engage both academic and public audiences.
approach: Led design across research interpretation, UX, and visualisation, shaping exploratory notebooks on Observable. Focused on simplifying narratives, refining interactions, and enabling layered exploration through iterative prototyping and user testing.
impact: Delivered public-facing research tools enabling exploration of complex historical datasets. Improved accessibility and engagement, supporting both academic analysis and wider public understanding of industrial-era change.
detail_1: Data-Driven Exploration of Industrial History || The Living with Machines project brought together institutions including the British Library and the Alan Turing Institute to explore how data science and machine learning could surface new perspectives on 19th-century history. Working within this multidisciplinary context, the focus was on translating complex datasets, particularly historical newspapers, into accessible and meaningful narratives that could support both research and public engagement. || ../img/project/livingwithmachines/zooinverse.jpg || Zooniverse crowdsourcing interface || Screenshot of the project on Zooniverse
detail_2: Structuring Research Through Interactive Analytical Tools || The work centred around two key strands, The Language of Accidents and The Language of Mechanisation, each designed as interactive notebooks that allowed users to explore patterns in data, from accident frequency and geography to the evolution of language over time. These tools balanced analytical depth with accessibility, enabling both expert and non-expert audiences to engage with the material. || ../img/project/livingwithmachines/DAVIZCT-workflow.jpg || Workflow diagram of crowdsourcing tasks || Example crowdsourcing workflow diagram
detail_3: Collaborative Design Bridging Research and User Needs || Working closely with researchers, I helped translate complex datasets such as crowdsourced annotations and newspaper metadata into early visual directions using Jupyter notebooks. This exploratory phase was complemented by collaborative mapping in Miro, aligning research goals with user needs and platform constraints to define a coherent interaction and visual strategy. || ../img/project/livingwithmachines/screenshots-jupyter-notebook.jpg || Jupyter notebook visualisations || Early data exploration in notebooks
detail_4: Aligning Design Direction Through Shared Mapping || Building on early explorations, Miro boards were used to map user journeys, interaction patterns, and design approaches, creating alignment across disciplines and ensuring that emerging solutions remained grounded in both research intent and usability. || ../img/project/livingwithmachines/Screenshot-miro-board1.jpg || Miro board design exploration || Research and design mapping process
detail_5: Iterative Testing to Refine Usability and Interpretation || Usability testing with small participant groups provided rapid feedback on comprehension and interaction, particularly around how users navigated between visualisations and original archival sources. These insights informed iterative refinements, strengthening the connection between data representation and source material. || ../img/project/livingwithmachines/Screenshot-miro-board2.jpg || User journeys and flows || User journeys and testing insights
detail_6: Delivering Accessible and Engaging Data Visualisations || The final outputs brought together interaction, narrative, and visual clarity, resulting in a set of Observable-based visualisations that made complex historical data more explorable and engaging for a broad audience. || ../img/project/livingwithmachines/collage.jpg || Final visualisations collage || Final visual outputs on Observable
detail_7: Enhancing Semantic Understanding Through Motion || To support interpretation within the Language of Mechanisation strand, I introduced animated icon sequences representing how meanings of words such as trolley, car, coach, and bike evolved over time, using motion to communicate change more intuitively than static visuals.  It further expanded this system, helping users build a mental model of how language evolves in response to technological and societal shifts || ../img/project/livingwithmachines/combined.gif || Trolley car coach bike icon animation || Top left to right: Car, Bus, Bottom left to right: Bike, Trolley

title: Red Riding Hood
thumbnail: ../img/project/preview/redridinghood-thumb.jpg || Data visualisation poster using geometric shapes to represent the Red Riding Hood story
hero: ../img/project/redridinghood/insitu.jpg  || Data visualisation poster using geometric shapes to represent the Red Riding Hood story
rationale: Story-driven data interpretation
oneliner: Narrative data visualisation using children’s literature as source material
lead: Explored narrative structures as a framework for mapping structured data relationships
context: A self-initiated project to develop my data visualisation skills, exploring how narrative data could be translated into a visual system. The brief emerged from a course, but quickly became a deeper personal exploration of style, process, and storytelling.
approach: I treated the story as a dataset, manually collecting and structuring information before translating it into a geometric visual language. Through sketching, iteration, and experimentation, I explored how characters, sentiment, and narrative structure could be represented spatially, balancing clarity with expressive form.
impact: Marked a significant step in developing my visual language and approach to data storytelling. The project surfaced both strengths and limitations in my process, shaping how I think about narrative, abstraction, and clarity in visualisation, and was recognised in the Information is Beautiful Awards longlist.
detail_1: Data Visualisation Exploration and Learning || I’ve been wanting to work on a data visualisation project for a while. I’ve been seeing more and more projects from others that had their own individual style and really wanted to try my hand at it to see what I could come up with. I had already dabbled with a few small pieces but nothing very polished. I knew it would also take me time to find my own style and get to a certain standard that I would be proud of. I’ve been immersing myself in data visualisation podcasts since they were a really accessible way for me to gain information with my tight schedule. || || ||
detail_2: Selecting a Data Source from Children's Literature || The course assignment started by choosing a book with a personal connection. I didn’t have one, so I explored public domain sources like Project Gutenberg. I selected Red Riding Hood by Lydia Very (1863), drawn to its unique shape, illustrations, and manageable length for manual data collection. || ../img/project/redridinghood/originalbook.jpg || Original book pages || Scanned pages from the Internet Archive
detail_3: Data Collection and Visualisation Planning || I manually collected data such as character presence, word counts, and narrative structure. The process was slow but rewarding, offering a deeper connection to the data. I then began sketching how the data could be visualised, aiming to reflect the story visually while using simple geometric shapes. || ../img/project/redridinghood/sketchbook.jpg || Sketchbook || Initial data collection and sketches
detail_4: Crafting a Geometric Storyscape || I developed a visual system where each page became a tree, forming a forest scene that mirrored the story setting. Characters were translated into geometric symbols, and layout explorations moved from linear to semi-circular to better reflect narrative flow. || ../img/project/redridinghood/flat.jpg || Flat layout || First version
detail_5: Exploring Sentiment and Refinement || I experimented with sentiment analysis to add another layer of meaning, identifying positive and negative words and representing them visually within the composition. Feedback from peers helped refine layout, clarity, and visual balance, while also surfacing issues such as attribution and readability. || ../img/project/redridinghood/monkeylearn.jpg || Sentiment tool || Sentiment analysis tool exploration

rationale: Structural complexity made legible through visual encoding

context: Complex workflows and technical processes across projects often created misalignment between teams and stakeholders. Visual artefacts were introduced as a way to bridge communication gaps, making systems, responsibilities, and processes easier to understand and align on.
approach: I facilitated collaborative diagramming sessions to extract and structure knowledge from cross-functional teams, translating it into clear, adaptable visual models. Through iterative refinement, these diagrams evolved into shared tools used to support planning, onboarding, and decision-making across projects.
impact: These visual models became shared reference points across teams, improving clarity, alignment, and efficiency in project planning and execution. By making complex systems legible, they supported better collaboration and more informed decision-making across technical and non-technical stakeholders.
detail_1: Driving Alignment Through Shared Visual Frameworks || To address fragmentation across projects, shared visual frameworks were established to align cross-functional teams and clarify workflows spanning internal teams and external partners. Rather than static diagrams, these functioned as evolving internal tools that made complex systems visible, actionable, and reusable across contexts. Developed through workshops and iterative refinement, they created a shared language across disciplines, reducing ambiguity in decision-making and enabling more consistent delivery. Over time, these artefacts became embedded in team workflows as reference points for planning, communication, and onboarding, supporting alignment at both project and organisational levels. || || ||
detail_2: Collaborative Modelling and Adoption Across Teams || Development was grounded in collaborative workshops, where perspectives from lab managers, analysts, and developers were synthesised into structured visual models. Iterative feedback loops ensured accuracy while maintaining clarity for different audiences, increasing trust and usability. This approach shifted diagrams from one-off outputs to actively used tools, supporting ongoing alignment, cross-team communication, and continuity across multiple projects. || || ||
detail_3: Mapping Internal and External Relationships || Visual models clarified how internal teams and external stakeholders interact, making dependencies, responsibilities, and communication flows explicit. This reduced friction in cross-team collaboration and supported more effective coordination across projects. || ../img/project/informationmodels/distribution.png || Diagram showing relationships between internal team and external stakeholders || Distribution model of internal and external relationships
detail_4: Aligning Delivery Through Lifecycle Models || Development lifecycle frameworks provided a shared view of delivery stages and dependencies, aligning cross-functional teams around how work progresses from concept to release. This improved coordination and reduced misunderstandings between design, engineering, and delivery roles. || ../img/project/informationmodels/SDLC-internal.jpg || Software development lifecycle diagram showing stages || Internal software development lifecycle model
detail_5: Clarifying Technical Architecture for Broader Understanding || Development stack visualisations translated technical architecture into accessible formats, enabling non-technical stakeholders to engage with system structure and constraints. This strengthened cross-functional decision-making and reduced reliance on siloed knowledge. || ../img/project/informationmodels/developmentstack.png || Development stack diagram || Development stack overview
detail_6: Establishing Service Standards and Expectations || Service-level agreement models defined responsibilities, expectations, and performance measures, supporting clearer alignment on delivery standards and improving operational consistency across teams. || ../img/project/informationmodels/slarecommendations.jpg || Diagram showing service level agreement structure || Service level agreement visual refinements
detail_7: Structuring Workflows and Delivery Planning || Timeline and work package visualisations improved visibility of tasks, dependencies, and milestones, enabling better coordination and more predictable delivery across projects. || ../img/project/informationmodels/workplan.png || Project workplan timeline diagram || Work package and project timeline diagram
detail_8: Making Iteration and Feedback Loops Visible || Iterative workflow models surfaced feedback cycles and continuous progression, helping teams better understand and manage ongoing development processes. This supported more adaptive and responsive ways of working. || ../img/project/informationmodels/ifu-miro.jpg || Iterative cycle diagram showing continuous progression || Iterative workflow and process cycles
detail_9: Embedding Accessibility into Delivery Workflows || Accessibility process visualisations integrated inclusive design practices into everyday workflows, shifting accessibility from a reactive consideration to a proactive, embedded part of delivery. || ../img/project/informationmodels/accessibility-overview.jpg || Accessibility process flowchart || Accessibility process overview
detail_10: Improving Operational Visibility and Task Tracking || Time and task tagging workflows increased transparency around workload, ownership, and progress, supporting better resource planning and accountability across teams. || ../img/project/informationmodels/timetasktag.jpg || Time and task tagging workflow diagram || Time and task tagging workflow
detail_11: Clarifying Roles and Decision-Making Structures || Responsibility mapping models made ownership and decision boundaries explicit, reducing ambiguity and enabling more efficient collaboration and governance across projects. || ../img/project/informationmodels/responsibility-percentage.jpg || Diagram showing role responsibilities distribution || Role responsibilities and distribution

title: Water Consumption
thumbnail: ../img/project/preview/waterconsumption-thumb.jpg || Wooden frames with lego scenarios and children's drawings with integrated data
hero: ../img/project/waterconsumption/insitu.jpg  || Wooden frames with lego scenarios and children's drawings with integrated data
rationale: Physical embodiment of quantitative data for insight
oneliner: Data physicalisation encouraging environmental awareness and behavioural reflection
output: Tiago::../project/waterconsumption-tiago.html || Silvia::../project/waterconsumption-silvia.html || Information is Beautiful 2022 (1)::https://www.informationisbeautifulawards.com/showcase/5702-water-consumption-1  || Information is Beautiful 2022 (2)::https://www.informationisbeautifulawards.com/showcase/5813-water-consumption-2
lead: Explored physicalisation methods to translate environmental data into tangible interaction forms
context: A personal project based on the very long showers my kids take. I wanted a way to explain to them how important water is and should be used sparingly but in a very simplified way that they could relate, understand and empathise with, so some elements and concepts might not be completely accurate. I was inspired by a lot by data comics and wanted to make something that had a similar feel.
approach: I worked collaboratively with my children to co-create two visualisations using mediums they were naturally drawn to. By grounding the data in their own habits and involving them in the making process, the project combined data physicalisation, storytelling, and visual design to foster understanding and emotional connection.
impact: Encouraged short-term behavioural change while demonstrating how participatory, creative approaches can make data more meaningful and accessible for younger audiences. The project also highlights the potential of combining individual interests, physical making with data storytelling to support early awareness of sustainability issues.
detail_1: Teaching Water Conservation || It started with a very ordinary parental frustration. Repeatedly asking my children to take shorter showers wasn't working, nor were the other usual methods, so I wanted to try a different approach to get them to understand the impact of water use. I also wanted to make it enjoyable both for them and myself, combining all our interests. || || ||
detail_2: Framing Everyday Water Usage as Measurable Behaviour || To ground the concept in familiar behaviours, I worked with my children to identify how water was used in everyday scenarios, narrowing this down to showering, washing hands, laundry, and flushing the toilet. I asked them how many times or how long they think they did each activity, and we wrote it down. With laundry it was the amount of clothes they would wash a week and converted roughly into the load per day. Together, we estimated frequency and duration, translating into daily usage to make it understandable. || || ||
detail_3: Designing Learning Through Favourite Mediums || The approach was tailored to how each child engaged with the world. Tiago was 6 years old at the time and obsessed with Lego, made the four scenarios with blocks parts all by himself. || ../img/project/waterconsumption/tiago-scenes.jpg || 4 photographs in a row of lego scenes and people || Lego  scenes:  washing hands,  flushing the toilet. showering, laundry
detail_4: Finding ways to create empathy || Silvia was 4 years old. She was the one in our household that took the longest showers, used the most clothes at the time so I had to think of a way for her to understand the impact of excess water use. I told her the water came from the sea and if she used too much, the animals in the sea would not have enough water to live and play in, connecting use to loss in a way she could feel, not just understand. || ../img/project/waterconsumption/silvia-scenes.jpg || child’s drawings and colouring || Drawing scenes:  washing hands,  flushing the toilet. showering, laundry, and ocean of sea creatures
detail_5: Fusing expertise and effort || I combined both of these outputs digitally, layering narrative, imagery, and simple data and text to create a cohesive visual system that connected behaviour to impact in a way that felt immediate and personal. With Tiago’s, I helped with stacking the blocks at the end to make it bar chart like, photographed each piece and compiled them in Photoshop with his help adding text and numbers, representing the rough volume of water used. Similar with Silvia’s, I photographed the drawings and put them together in Photoshop, placed the sea drawing at the top and added some blocks of blue shades to depict water flowing from the sea into the home. I added the text and numbers. || ../img/project/waterconsumption/unframed.jpg || Lego and drawing based water consumption visualisations || Left: Lego-based scenarios and bar charts. Right: Illustrated scenarios showing water flow from sea to home.
detail_6: Short-Term Engagement and Behaviour Change  || The immediate impact was clear, behaviour shifted quickly. Their showers were incredibly quick and it became a bit of a race on who could have the shortest ones but unfortunately after a few weeks they didn’t seem to care much anymore and reverted back. But I hope they will hold the memory of this project we did together and grow to understand and appreciate the value of water better as they grow older.|| || ||

title: Family Tree
thumbnail: ../img/project/preview/familytree-thumb.jpg || Tablet displaying a genealogy data visualisation in a structure chart format
hero: ../img/project/familytree/insitu.jpg  || Tablet displaying a genealogy data visualisation in a structure chart format
rationale: Hierarchical data representation
oneliner: Multi-generational relationship mapping revealing structural patterns
lead: Explored hierarchical relationship structures through genealogical data modelling
context: Traditional family trees often prioritise hierarchy over time, limiting the ability to uncover patterns across generations. This project explored how combining relational structures with timelines could surface more nuanced insights, while working with incomplete and privacy-sensitive data.
approach: A personal project translating a fragmented dataset into a structured visual system, mapping individuals by birth date while preserving relational connections across generations. Through sketching and iterative layout testing, I explored how spatial positioning and minimal visual encoding could reveal trends without relying on rigid axes or complete data.
impact: The project demonstrated how even incomplete personal data can be structured to reveal meaningful patterns when visualised thoughtfully. It also established a flexible model for combining relational and temporal data, forming a foundation for future interactive and scalable implementations.
detail_1: Exploring Visualisation of Relational and Incomplete Data || The project investigated how to represent multi-generational family data through a hybrid model combining timelines and relationships. Designed under constraints of incomplete and sensitive data, it explored alternative approaches to traditional hierarchical family trees. || || ||
detail_2: Working with Evolving and Partial Datasets || The dataset originated from a long-term spreadsheet with gaps due to privacy considerations, requiring a flexible approach to representation. The goal was to surface patterns across four generations despite incomplete information. || ../img/project/familytree/sketches.jpg || Sketches exploring layout ideas for family tree visualisation || Early sketches exploring structure and layout
detail_3: Designing a Flexible Visual System || Individuals were positioned along a vertical timeline based on birth date, while relationships were expressed without a fixed horizontal axis. This prioritised relational clarity over strict quantitative structure, enabling more expressive representation of connections. || ../img/project/familytree/mockups.jpg || Mockups testing layout and relationships between nodes || Mockups used to test layout and relationships
detail_4: Refining and Validating the Final Model || Iteration resulted in a final chart that balances clarity, flexibility, and privacy, demonstrating how alternative structures can reveal patterns not visible in traditional models. || ../img/project/familytree/final-chart.jpg || Final family tree chart with blurred names || Final chart with anonymised data
detail_5: Future Direction and System Potential || The next stage involves translating the static visual into an interactive system, enabling dynamic updates, filtering, and exploration as the dataset evolves, positioning it as a living model rather than a fixed artefact. || || ||

rationale: Structured communication of complex social data
oneliner: Prototype platform communicating social impact through SDG-aligned narratives
partners: Entrepreneurship Institute::https://www.kcl.ac.uk/entrepreneurship || Time Partners Ltd::https://timepartners.co.uk || Alter Equity::https://www.alterequity.com || GO Lab, University of Oxford::https://golab.bsg.ox.ac.uk || King's Business School::https://www.kcl.ac.uk/business || Faculty of Social Science & Public Policy::https://www.kcl.ac.uk/sspp
lead: Designed narrative system for translating impact data into structured communicative framework
context: Social impact measurement across business activities is often fragmented, inconsistent, and difficult to interpret. This project explored how a unified platform could support clearer analysis and communication of impact data across policymakers, businesses, and the public along with unifying analysis and reporting into a more accessible, transparent system for diverse audiences.
approach: I led a user-centred design process to translate complex analytical requirements into a structured, navigable product experience. This included defining key user groups, mapping task flows, and designing interaction models that supported both high-level insights and deeper exploration. Through iterative prototyping, I aligned research, data structures, and interface design into a cohesive reporting system that balanced clarity with analytical depth.
impact: Delivered a working prototype that reframed social impact reporting as an interactive, user-driven experience. The platform concept demonstrated how complex datasets could be made accessible and comparable, supporting better decision-making and engagement across sectors. It also established a scalable foundation for future product development in impact measurement.
detail_1: Sketching ideas and data wrangling || I explored multiple layouts and data transformations, including adapting RAWGraphs outputs into circular structures and refining them in Illustrator. || ../img/project/rrh2/sketchbook.jpg || Sketchbook showing layout and data exploration ideas || Concept sketches and layout exploration
detail_2: Target Audience and Conceptualisation || At the outset of the design process, the team engaged in extensive brainstorming sessions to define the vision and objectives of the ERR platform. This phase involved identifying key stakeholders, understanding their needs and pain points, and establishing the overarching goals for the project. Policymakers, members of the public, academics, auditors, and business leaders form the diverse spectrum of target audiences for the ERR platform. The platform aims to cater to these stakeholders by providing comprehensive and accessible information on business activities and their social impact. || || ||
detail_3: Task Flow Analysis || Following the conceptualization phase, the team delved into task flow analysis to map out the various user interactions and pathways within the ERR platform. This involved identifying the primary tasks that users would need to accomplish and determining the most intuitive and efficient flow for achieving these tasks. || ../img/project/illustratingimpact/ill-0.jpg || Task flow diagram showing frontend and admin pathways || Diagram of task flow between frontend and admin pages
detail_4: Understanding User Journeys || With a clear understanding of the user tasks and interactions, the team proceeded to develop journey maps to visualize the end-to-end user experience on the ERR platform. These journey maps outlined the steps users would take to accomplish their goals, highlighting key touchpoints, pain points, and opportunities for improvement. || ../img/project/illustratingimpact/ill-1.jpg || User journey map showing platform pages and interactions || Diagram of journey map showing pages
detail_5: Mockup Development and Refinements || Armed with insights from task flow analysis and journey mapping, the team began crafting mockups to visualize the user interface and interactive reporting features of the ERR platform. These mockups served as prototypes for the platform, allowing iteration, stakeholder feedback, and refinement before development. || ../img/project/illustratingimpact/ill-2.jpg || Grayscale mockups of multiple platform pages || Mockups of the ERR platform interface

title: Archetype

oneliner: Digital tool for exploring and preserving medieval manuscript collections
funder: European Research Council::https://erc.europa.eu || Arts and Humanities Research Council (AHRC)::https://www.ukri.org/councils/ahrc/ || King's College London::https://www.kcl.ac.uk

It also established a repeatable approach for translating technical systems into accessible digital experiences.
detail_1: Translating a Complex Research Framework into an Accessible Platform || Archetype is an open-source system for analysing palaeographical materials, requiring a clear and accessible web presence to communicate its value. The project focused on translating a complex technical framework into a lightweight, user-facing platform under tight constraints. || || ||
detail_2: Designing for Speed and Clarity Under Constraints || A pragmatic approach used a pre-existing one-page template to accelerate development, allowing focus on content structure, layout, and user experience rather than building from scratch. These visual elements improved accessibility and engagement with complex content. || ../img/project/archetype/workflow-mockup.jpg || Mockup of the Archetype workflow model || Workflow model exploration
detail_4: Refining Visual Models for Clarity and Use || Iteration on workflow diagrams ensured clarity and usability, resulting in a final model that effectively communicates system structure and processes. || ../img/project/archetype/workflow-final.png || Final version of the Archetype workflow model || Final workflow model

title: Athena Swan
thumbnail: ../img/project/preview/athenaswan-thumb.jpg || Laptop displaying a snakes and ladders game grid with animal illustrations

rationale: Research reframed through interaction and insight
oneliner: Gender equity research findings, reframed as an interactive infographic people chose to explore
lead: Designed an interactive engaging  infographic to communicate  research insights on gender equality
context: Humanities departments working towards Athena SWAN accreditation faced challenges in translating qualitative research and institutional practices into clear, shareable formats. Existing outputs were often report-heavy and difficult to engage with beyond academic audiences. This project explored how research findings and best practices could be communicated more effectively through a digital, interactive format.
approach: I designed an interactive infographic that reframed research insights into a narrative-driven experience, balancing clarity with engagement under tight time and budget constraints. Drawing on collaborative workshops with researchers, I translated interview and focus group findings into a structured visual system, using a game-based metaphor to guide users through progress and setbacks. impact: The interactive format demonstrated how qualitative research and institutional practices could be translated into a more engaging and accessible experience for broader audiences. By reframing dense report content into a narrative, visual system, the project expanded the reach and usability of the findings beyond academic contexts. It also provides a replicable approach for communicating complex research through lightweight, interactive design.

lead: Translated archival acquisition data into structured visual mapping system
context: Existing project materials highlighted a gap between the intended narrative, illustrating relationships between Shakespeare, the royals, and collected objects, and what was represented in the interface. Using dedicated exploration time, I revisited the data and project goals to investigate how visualisation could better support this core argument.
approach: I iteratively explored multiple visual models, manually plotting data and testing different ways to balance depth, clarity, and narrative. Each version refined how relationships, timelines, and object counts were represented, using feedback and feasibility to guide simplification and structure. The process prioritised experimentation, progressively reducing complexity to identify more legible and scalable approaches.
impact: The exploration surfaced multiple viable approaches for visualising complex historical relationships, highlighting tradeoffs between detail, clarity, and scalability. It demonstrated how iterative prototyping can uncover structural challenges early, informing more effective design decisions for future implementation once complete data becomes available.
detail_1: Exploring Visualisation of Complex Cultural Data || The project explored how to represent relationships between Shakespeare, royal figures, and associated artefacts, identifying gaps in the existing platform where these connections were not clearly communicated. detail_2: Iterating Through Visual Exploration || Early concepts were developed through sketches and mockups, manually plotting data points to understand structure, hierarchy, and relationships. This exploratory phase established a foundation for testing different visual approaches to representing complex historical data. || ../img/project/sharc/sketches.jpg || Digitally drawn sketches exploring data visualisation concepts || Early sketches exploring layout and structure
detail_3: Testing Layered Timeline Approaches || The first iteration introduced a layered timeline combining lifespans, acquisitions, and key events to provide a comprehensive overview. While conceptually rich, feedback highlighted challenges with clarity and overlap, particularly when multiple data layers competed for attention. || ../img/project/sharc/prototype1.jpg || First prototype showing layered timeline visualisation || Version 1
detail_4: Simplifying Structure for Clarity || The second iteration reduced complexity by focusing on clearer relationships between individuals and datasets. This improved readability but introduced new challenges when scaling across larger datasets, highlighting tradeoffs between clarity and completeness. || ../img/project/sharc/prototype2.jpg || Second prototype with simplified structure || Version 2
detail_5: Moving Toward Modular and Scalable Systems || The third iteration introduced modular components grouped by time and volume, improving clarity and visual organisation. detail_6: Refining Modular Visualisation Concepts || Further refinement of the modular approach explored how layout, grouping, and visual cues could support scalable interaction while maintaining clarity across complex datasets. || ../img/project/sharc/prototype3.jpg || Third prototype with modular layout || Version 3
