lead: Contributed to large-scale archival system enabling structured access to historical publications
context: The exisiting platform hosts over 400,000 articles from nineteenth-century periodicals, requiring a system capable of supporting both large-scale content management and scholarly exploration. A redesign was initiated to improve usability and functionality, guided by historical user feedback but constrained by limited resources and the absence of a working legacy system.
approach: I focused on restructuring key user journeys, particularly “explore” and “search”, to make navigation across a large dataset more intuitive and efficient. Working from screenshots of the previous system, I balanced preserving familiar visual elements with improving accessibility and interaction patterns, while also introducing data visualisation to support higher-level content understanding.
impact: Researchers gained a more navigable and interpretable interface for exploring a large and complex archive. The redesign demonstrated how incremental, user-informed improvements can significantly enhance usability even under technical and resource constraints, while introducing data-driven views that support new forms of scholarly exploration.
detail_1: Transforming a Large-Scale Archival Discovery System || The NCSE project involved redesigning a large-scale digital archive containing over 400,000 historical articles across multiple publications spanning 84 years. Originally built for scholarly research, the platform required rethinking as a search and discovery system capable of supporting both targeted queries and exploratory browsing. The redesign focused on improving usability and access to complex archival data, balancing user needs with technical and resource constraints while maintaining the integrity of the underlying dataset. || || ||
detail_2: Designing Without a Functional Legacy System || With the original platform no longer operational, the redesign relied on static screenshots as the only reference point. This constraint required reconstructing user flows and identifying usability issues without direct interaction, revealing significant challenges in navigation clarity and content access. This approach shifted the process from iterative optimisation to critical reinterpretation, establishing a clearer foundation for restructuring the experience. || ../img/project/ncse/screenshot-oldsite.jpg || Screenshots of the previous NCSE website || Reference screenshots of the previous platform
detail_3: Restructuring Navigation for Search and Exploration || The platform structure was redesigned to support clearer pathways between “search” and “explore,” reducing friction in how users accessed content. Navigation was simplified to better reflect user intent, enabling both direct retrieval of known items and broader discovery of archival material. This restructuring improved orientation within the system and reduced cognitive load when navigating large datasets. || ../img/project/ncse/sitemap.jpg || Sitemap showing structure of redesigned platform || Sitemap of redesigned information architecture
detail_4: Balancing Familiarity with Improved Accessibility || While introducing structural improvements, key visual elements such as colour and style were retained to maintain continuity for returning users. Accessibility reviews informed refinements to layout, contrast, and interaction patterns, ensuring the experience was more inclusive without disrupting familiarity. || ../img/project/ncse/illustrations.jpg || Adapted illustrations used within the interface || Adapted illustrations integrated into the interface
detail_5: Enhancing Discovery Through Data Visualisation || Data visualisation was introduced to surface patterns within the archive, supporting exploratory use cases beyond traditional search. Timeline views and publication-level charts provided insight into content distribution, including text, images, and advertisements, enabling users to engage with the dataset at both macro and micro levels. || ../img/project/ncse/mockup-charts.jpg || Mockups of data visualisation charts for the archive || Early mockups of data visualisation concepts
detail_6: Outcome and Impact on Research Experience || The redesigned platform improved navigation clarity, accessibility, and overall usability, enabling more efficient access to archival content. By integrating structured navigation with exploratory tools such as data visualisation, the system better supports a range of research behaviours, from precise lookup to open-ended discovery, strengthening its role as both a research tool and a knowledge exploration platform. || || ||

lead: Designer on a tablet newspaper app redesign,  working within strict brand guidelines, at commercial pace, for a conservative audience making their first move from print to digital.
context: The Mirror newspaper app for tablets was underperforming — users weren't engaging, subscribing, or getting value from the tablet format. Working at pace within strict brand guidelines and for a conservative newspaper audience, the challenge was to improve the experience without making the change feel jarring.
approach: I led the design of the icon system and visual language, and the help screens and onboarding flow — the two areas most directly affecting first impressions and ongoing usability. Decisions were grounded in informal user feedback and guided by a creative director. Accessibility ran through the work throughout: visuals and content had to meet audience needs across a wide range of users and contexts.
impact: The redesigned app saw improvements across downloads, subscriptions, and engagement metrics including time in app and pages read. A measurable result in a fast-moving commercial environment, working within some of the tightest brand constraints I've encountered.

title: Interactive Maps
thumbnail: ../img/project/preview/mapwidget-thumb.jpg || hand holding mobile device in front of a laptop screen, both showing the same watercoloured styled map with a list on the side or below
hero: ../img/project/mapwidget/insitu.jpg || hand holding mobile device in front of a laptop screen, both showing the same watercoloured styled map with a list on the side or below
rationale: Reusable interactive system for location-based data storytelling
oneliner: Interactive mapping component enabling scalable location-based storytelling
output: Reusable interactive map component used across editorial content
lead: Designed and developed a reusable map-based interface for structuring and exploring geospatial editorial content
context: The Editorial team at Trinity Mirror (now Reach plc) needed a consistent way to present location-based stories across multiple titles. Existing approaches were largely static or bespoke, leading to inconsistent user experiences and repeated development effort.
approach: I designed and built a modular interactive map component that combined geospatial navigation with structured content. The solution prioritised usability across devices, reusability across teams, and flexibility for different editorial contexts.
impact: Delivered a reusable component that reduced the need for one-off map builds and enabled consistent interactive storytelling across articles. The system improved editorial efficiency and established a scalable foundation for future data-driven features.
detail_1: Framing the problem through research || I began by reviewing how leading news and data platforms approached map-based storytelling, alongside identifying common usability issues—particularly on mobile where precision interactions are limited. This highlighted the need for an approach that balanced spatial exploration with straightforward content access. || ../img/project/mapwidget/research.jpg || Collage of visual research || Visual research exploring map interaction patterns and layouts
detail_2: Designing a dual interaction model || To reduce reliance on map-only interaction, I introduced a hybrid interface: a scrollable list of locations for quick scanning, and an interactive map with markers for spatial context.Both views were fully synchronised—selecting a list item or marker triggered the same content, improving accessibility and usability across devices. || ../img/project/mapwidget/wireframe.jpg || Drawing of wireframe || Wireframe defining the relationship between list and map interactions
detail_3: Building a reusable, flexible component || To reduce reliance on map-only interaction, I implemented the solution using Leaflet, allowing for: lightweight performance suitable for news platforms; flexible configuration of markers and data sources; support for multiple map tile providers. To increase adaptability, I created a small internal library of map tile styles, enabling the component to visually align with different story types without redesign. This shifted the output from a one-off feature to a repeatable product capability. The component was designed to perform consistently across screen sizes, ensuring usability on both desktop and mobile without feature loss. || ../img/project/mapwidget/example1.jpg || Desktop and mobile view of map widget || Responsive behaviour across desktop and mobile
detail_4: Extending into editorial workflows || Beyond the interface, I considered how the component would integrate into editorial processes. The data structure was designed to support future integration with tools such as Google Sheets, enabling: easier content entry and updates; reduced reliance on developer support; scalability across multiple stories and teams This positioned the component not just as a front-end feature, but as part of a broader content production system. || ../img/project/mapwidget/example2.jpg || Desktop and mobile view of map widget || Consistent interaction patterns maintained across devices

rationale: Sequential visualisation of location-based narrative

approach: I translated the itinerary into a simplified visual narrative using an animated map. Rather than introducing interactive complexity, I focused on a passive, linear format that aligned with editorial constraints and user consumption patterns.
impact: Delivered a lightweight, easily deployable visual that clearly communicated a multi-stop itinerary without requiring development overhead. The approach balanced clarity, speed, and editorial practicality.
detail_1: Framing the editorial need || The requirement was to communicate a sequence of locations in a way that was more engaging than text, but without introducing interaction or additional user effort. detail_2: Choosing simplicity over interactivity || Rather than developing an interactive map, I created a linear animated sequence that guided the viewer through each stop on the tour. This removed the need for navigation controls and ensured the story could be consumed passively, which was more appropriate for the editorial context and audience behaviour. ||
detail_3: Maintaining brand consistency across teams || Each publication had distinct branding requirements, including Mirror Celebs, regional titles, and sports sections. I ensured consistency in structure while allowing visual variation through controlled styling tokens and brand-specific adjustments. ||
detail_4: Producing a lightweight visual solution || The animation was created using a GIF-based approach in Photoshop, allowing for rapid production and seamless integration into the publishing workflow. title: Nepalese Nettle
thumbnail: ../img/project/preview/dunsmore-thumb.jpg || Two balls of handspun brown nettle yarn
hero: ../img/project/dunsmore/insitu.jpg  || Two balls of handspun brown nettle yarn
rationale: Practice-led material systems exploration
oneliner: Field research and workshops exploring sustainable craft and material practices
team: Craft practitioners, Field collaborators, Local community
lead: Conducted material research exploring sustainable craft systems through field-based practice
context: As part of the John Dunsmore Travel Scholarship, this project focused on developing a product using locally sourced nettle fibre in a remote region of Nepal. The challenge was to balance market viability with existing skills, cultural practices, and the practical realities of working within a short timeframe alongside local artisans.
approach: I worked directly with a group of Nepalese women to understand their making processes and constraints, adapting my approach in real time based on their preferences and working styles. Rather than introducing entirely new techniques, I focused on evolving existing knitting skills into a new product direction, rapidly prototyping and iterating through hands-on collaboration.
impact: Demonstrated how existing craft practices could be adapted into new product directions without disrupting local workflows. The collaborative process surfaced practical, scalable approaches to designing with communities, reinforcing the value of working within cultural and material constraints rather than imposing external solutions.
detail_1: Working with Local Materials and Craft Traditions || This project was developed through the John Dunsmore Travel Scholarship during my time at the Royal College of Art, providing an opportunity to spend seven weeks in north-east Nepal working with a group of women producing textiles from locally sourced nettle fibre. The brief was to develop a product using this material that could be suitable for local use, tourism, or export, grounding design decisions in both context and practicality. || || ||
detail_2: Reaching the Weaving Centre in a Remote Context || The journey to the weaving centre in Sankhuwasabha District highlighted the remoteness of the location and the conditions in which the work would take place, involving travel from Kathmandu by plane, jeep, and several days of trekking. This context shaped both the pace of the project and the importance of working with available resources and existing practices. || ../img/project/dunsmore/theweavingcentre.jpg || Photograph of the Weaving Centre || The Weaving Centre
detail_3: Understanding Existing Craft Practices and Preferences || Initial research included exploring craft shops in Kathmandu and observing the range of products already being made, from belts and bags to garments. Through discussion with the women, it became clear that knitting, particularly shawl making, was preferred due to its portability and ability to fit around daily responsibilities, unlike weaving which required fixed equipment. || ../img/project/dunsmore/craftshop.jpg || Photograph of a craft shop || Knitted shawls hanging on display at a local shop
detail_4: Collaborating Through Making Rather Than Instruction || Early attempts at collaborative ideation through drawing were less effective, leading to a shift towards hands-on experimentation. The group naturally gravitated towards knitting, and I adapted by leading a larger knitting group, working alongside them to explore possibilities through making rather than prescriptive direction. || ../img/project/dunsmore/knittinggroup.jpg || Photograph of the knitting group || The knitting group
detail_5: Defining a Product Within Real Constraints || The product needed to respond to multiple constraints, including suitability for market, alignment with existing skills, and the ability to be completed within a limited timeframe. These considerations shaped a pragmatic design approach, focusing on what could realistically be achieved while still creating something meaningful and adaptable. || ../img/project/dunsmore/knittedshawls.jpg || Photograph of a knitted shawls || Folded stack of knitted shawls
detail_6: Translating Familiar Forms into New Products || Observing that many women carried woven bags led to the idea of creating a knitted bag, translating a familiar and functional object into a new format using existing knitting expertise. This built on established behaviours and preferences while introducing variation in product output. || ../img/project/dunsmore/wovenbags.jpg || Photograph of a knitted shawls || Personal collection of the local women's colourful traditional woven bags
detail_7: Adapting Materials and Techniques Creatively || To introduce variation without complex processes such as dyeing, coloured fabric was used as backing for lace knits, enhancing pattern visibility while preserving the natural qualities of the nettle fibre. This approach balanced aesthetic exploration with practical constraints. || ../img/project/dunsmore/laceonfabric.jpg || Photograph a knitted lace backed with coloured fabric || Knitted lace with natural nettle backed with coloured fabric
detail_8: Learning and Integrating Local Techniques || The project incorporated additional techniques such as plaited straps inspired by traditional basket-making, which required learning methods typically practiced by men in the community. This exchange of knowledge added both structural and cultural depth to the final product. || ../img/project/dunsmore/wovenstrap.jpg || Photograph of woven bamboo strap on traditional rattan like baskets || Woven bamboo strap on traditional rattan like baskets
detail_9: Prototyping Through Improvisation and Iteration || Prototyping involved adapting available materials and tools, including improvising knitting needles from sharpened wooden sticks and experimenting with construction methods such as circular knitting to reduce assembly time. A sample bag was created by repurposing an existing shawl, combining lining and strap techniques into a working prototype. || ../img/project/dunsmore/samplebag.jpg || Photograph a sample bag || Sample bag I put together by cutting up a shawl
detail_10: Refining Techniques Through Shared Practice || The process of making, including learning and demonstrating techniques such as casting on and strap weaving, became a collaborative activity, with knowledge shared across the group and refined through repetition and experimentation. || ../img/project/dunsmore/castingon.jpg || Photograph of some women casting on || Some women casting on with bamboo stcks
detail_11: Delivering a Viable and Adaptable Product || The first completed bag demonstrated the viability of the concept, with subsequent versions produced by the group. The initial pieces were intentionally small to ensure completion within the timeframe, with potential for scaling and adaptation after the project concluded. || ../img/project/dunsmore/firstbag.jpg || Photograph of the first finished bag || The first finished bag!

title: Entomological Knits
thumbnail: ../img/project/preview/entomologicalknits-thumb.jpg || Close up of small knitted pieces pinned down
hero: ../img/project/entomologicalknits/insitu.jpg  || Close up of small knitted pieces pinned down
rationale: Material experimentation and design exploration
oneliner: Experimental craft work exploring knitting techniques through contemporary design
lead: Explored experimental material practices through textile-based design research
context: This project was developed as a final exhibition piece exploring how traditional crafts can be reframed within a contemporary context. It focused on knitting, a practice often associated with function, to highlight its overlooked technical and aesthetic qualities. The work positions craft as both cultural heritage and a form of artistic expression.
approach: The project reinterpreted knitting by isolating and presenting its structural elements as standalone artefacts. Drawing inspiration from museum displays, each piece was framed and labelled to encourage close observation and deeper understanding of technique. This approach combined material exploration with narrative framing to shift how the work is perceived.
impact: The exhibition created a new lens through which knitting could be understood, encouraging viewers to engage with it as both an artistic and cultural practice. By isolating and elevating technical details, the work prompted reflection on the value of craft in a contemporary context. It also demonstrated how presentation and framing can reshape perception of familiar materials.
detail_1: Reframing Knitting as Contemporary Craft || This project explored how knitting can be repositioned from a functional craft to a form of contemporary artistic expression, highlighting the intricacies and technical beauty often overlooked within finished garments. The aim was to spark curiosity and encourage viewers to reconsider knitting as an independent visual and cultural medium. ||  ||  ||
detail_2: Inspired by Craft Heritage and Technical Detail || Developed as a final project at the Royal College of Art, the work draws from traditional knitting techniques and vintage patterns, focusing on elements such as sock heels, gussets, ribs, and pockets. By isolating these details, the project brings attention to the craftsmanship embedded within functional forms that are often hidden in everyday use. || ../img/project/entomologicalknits/closeups.jpg || closeups 3 frames of wall pieces of entomological knits || Close up of Molt Balls, Seven Ribs, Square Heels
detail_3: Museum-Inspired Display as Narrative Device || To shift perception, the pieces were presented in a museum-like format, displayed behind glass with accompanying labels describing the techniques used. Removing the tactile aspect encouraged viewers to observe more closely, treating each knitted form as an artefact and inviting deeper engagement with both its visual and technical qualities. || ../img/project/entomologicalknits/wallpieces.jpg || wall pieces of entomological knits || Wall pieces: Shelf of jars, Three frames
detail_4: Exploring Repetition and Variation in Craft || Each element was produced in multiple colour variations, referencing contemporary manufacturing processes where products are replicated across different finishes. This introduced a contrast between the labour-intensive nature of handcraft and the visual language of mass production, reinforcing the relationship between tradition and modernity. || ../img/project/entomologicalknits/tablepieces.jpg || table pieces of entomological knits || Table pieces: Three Heel Sock, Spatchcock Sock, Rib Rib Rib
detail_5: Isolating Craft to Reveal Form and Detail || By removing knitting from its conventional context within garments, the work allowed each stitch, seam, and construction technique to be appreciated independently. This shift in framing created space for viewers to engage with the material at a slower pace, recognising both its precision and expressive qualities. || ../img/project/entomologicalknits/3wallframes.jpg || 3 frames of wall pieces of entomological knits || Wall frame collection: Molt Balls, Seven Ribs, Square Heels
